meaning: must do; have to; gotta (casual contraction of 〜なくては)

exercises.example_sentence: 明日は早いから、もう寝なくちゃ。
exercises.translation: I have an early start tomorrow, so I've gotta go to bed.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form ('not sleeping, ...'), used to link clauses; it does not express the obligation 'must sleep' that the speaker is conveying here.
なくても means 'even if (I) don't,' a concessive — that would say 'even if I don't sleep, ...' which is the opposite vibe of needing to sleep.
ないで means 'without doing' (manner), not 'must do'; 'ないで' before nothing also doesn't end a sentence as a self-directed obligation.
exercises.hiragana_full: あしたははやいから、もうねなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 明日早いし、そろそろ寝なきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あしたはやいし、そろそろねなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: もう寝なくちゃいけない時間だ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: もうねなくちゃいけないじかんだ。

exercises.example_sentence: 明日テストだから、勉強しなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: I have a test tomorrow, so I gotta study.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて gives the negative te-form ('not studying, ...'), a connector that doesn't carry the 'must study' meaning the speaker needs before a test.
なくても means 'even if I don't study,' a concession that flatly contradicts the speaker's intent of getting ready for tomorrow's test.
ないで means 'without studying' (manner) — useful for things like 勉強しないで寝た, but it can't end the sentence as a self-imposed obligation.
exercises.hiragana_full: あしたてすとだから、べんきょうしなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 明日テストだし、勉強しなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あしたてすとだし、べんきょうしなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: テスト前だから、ちゃんと勉強しなくちゃね。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: てすとまえだから、ちゃんとべんきょうしなくちゃね。

exercises.example_sentence: もう時間がない、急いで行かなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: There's no time left — I gotta hurry up and go.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form, a clause connector ('not going, ...'); it doesn't carry the 'must go' urgency the speaker wants here.
なくても means 'even if I don't go,' which contradicts the speaker's panic about being late.
ないで means 'without going,' a manner adverbial — it can't function as the self-directed 'I've got to' that ends this sentence.
exercises.hiragana_full: もうじかんがない、いそいでいかなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 時間がないから、早く行かなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: じかんがないから、はやくいかなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: もう遅れそうだ、急いで行かなくちゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: もうおくれそうだ、いそいでいかなくちゃ。

exercises.example_sentence: 牛乳が切れてるから、買わなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: We're out of milk, so I gotta buy some.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form for linking clauses ('not buying, ...'); the speaker means 'must buy,' which is 〜なくちゃ.
なくても means 'even if I don't buy,' which is the opposite of the speaker's plan to restock the milk.
ないで means 'without buying' (manner), useful for sequencing actions but not for ending a sentence with a self-directed obligation.
exercises.hiragana_full: ぎゅうにゅうがきれてるから、かわなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 牛乳がもうないから、買わなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ぎゅうにゅうがもうないから、かわなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 牛乳が切れたので、買いに行かなくちゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: ぎゅうにゅうがきれたので、かいにいかなくちゃ。

exercises.example_sentence: 部屋が汚いから、掃除しなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: My room is dirty, so I've gotta clean it.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form for joining clauses; it doesn't express the obligation 'must clean' that fits the dirty room.
なくても means 'even if I don't clean,' a concession that contradicts the speaker's plan to tidy up.
ないで means 'without cleaning' (manner), not 'I've got to clean'; it also can't stand at sentence end as a self-directed must.
exercises.hiragana_full: へやがきたないから、そうじしなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 部屋が散らかってるし、片付けなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: へやがちらかってるし、かたづけなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 部屋が汚いので、そろそろ掃除しなくちゃね。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: へやがきたないので、そろそろそうじしなくちゃね。

exercises.example_sentence: そろそろ母に電話しなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: It's about time I called my mom.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form ('not calling, ...'), a connector — not the 'should/must' meaning the speaker needs.
なくても means 'even if I don't call,' a concession that contradicts the speaker's sense that the call is overdue.
ないで means 'without calling,' a manner adverbial; it can't end the sentence as a self-directed obligation to call.
exercises.hiragana_full: そろそろははにでんわしなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: そろそろお母さんに電話しなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: そろそろおかあさんにでんわしなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: しばらく話してないし、母に電話しなくちゃね。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: しばらくはなしてないし、ははにでんわしなくちゃね。

exercises.example_sentence: この本、明日までに図書館に返さなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: I've gotta return this book to the library by tomorrow.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form, used to link clauses ('not returning, ...'); it doesn't carry the obligation 'must return.'
なくても means 'even if I don't return it,' which would let the speaker off the hook — the opposite of meeting the deadline.
ないで means 'without returning' (manner), not 'must return'; it also doesn't function as a sentence-final self-directed must.
exercises.hiragana_full: このほん、あしたまでにとしょかんにかえさなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 明日が返却日だから、図書館に返さなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: あしたがへんきゃくびだから、としょかんにかえさなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: この本は期限が明日までだから、返さなくちゃいけない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: このほんはきげんがあしたまでだから、かえさなくちゃいけない。

exercises.example_sentence: 今夜中に宿題を終わらせなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: I've got to finish my homework by the end of tonight.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて gives the negative te-form ('not finishing, ...'), a clause connector — it doesn't express the urgency 'must finish.'
なくても means 'even if I don't finish,' which contradicts the deadline pressure the speaker is describing.
ないで means 'without finishing' (manner) and can't stand at the end as a self-imposed obligation.
exercises.hiragana_full: こんやじゅうにしゅくだいをおわらせなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 今夜のうちに宿題を終わらせなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: こんやのうちにしゅくだいをおわらせなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 今晩中に課題を仕上げなくちゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: こんばんじゅうにかだいをしあげなくちゃ。

exercises.example_sentence: 昨日のこと、友達に謝らなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: I've gotta apologize to my friend for yesterday.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form ('not apologizing, ...'), a clause connector, not the 'must apologize' meaning required here.
なくても means 'even if I don't apologize,' which contradicts the speaker's resolve to make things right.
ないで means 'without apologizing' (manner); it cannot serve as the sentence-final obligation the speaker is expressing.
exercises.hiragana_full: きのうのこと、ともだちにあやまらなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 昨日のこと、ちゃんと友達に謝らなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: きのうのこと、ちゃんとともだちにあやまらなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 昨日のことについて、友達にきちんと謝らなくちゃいけない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: きのうのことについて、ともだちにきちんとあやまらなくちゃいけない。

exercises.example_sentence: 電車に間に合わないから、急がなくちゃ。
exercises.translation: I won't make the train, so I've gotta hurry.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくて is the negative te-form for linking clauses ('not hurrying, ...'); it doesn't convey the urgency 'must hurry' that the train deadline calls for.
なくても means 'even if I don't hurry,' a concession that contradicts the speaker's worry about catching the train.
ないで means 'without hurrying' (manner) — useful inside a longer clause, but it can't stand at the end as the self-directed must the sentence needs.
exercises.hiragana_full: でんしゃにまにあわないから、いそがなくちゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 電車に乗り遅れちゃうから、急がなきゃ。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: でんしゃにのりおくれちゃうから、いそがなきゃ。

audio_alternatives.kanji: 電車の時間ぎりぎりだ、急がなくちゃ間に合わない。
audio_alternatives.hiragana_full: でんしゃのじかんぎりぎりだ、いそがなくちゃまにあわない。
